exemplar.title:
To what extent do the design and implementation of braking
systems contribute to reducing g-forces experienced by Formula 1 drivers during
high-speed maneuvers?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent do the design and implementation of braking
systems contribute to reducing g-forces experienced by Formula 1 drivers during
high-speed maneuvers?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The topic is communicated with clarity, precision and sustained focus, with consistent sign-posting and effective use of headings.
The research question is explicit, narrowly focused and continuously aligned with the discussion.
The methodology is described in sufficient detail for replication, with sensible use of primary and secondary data.
weaknesses: Key methodological choices (linear scaling of g-forces, control variables, outlier treatment) are noted only briefly and introduce unphysical values.
Justification for the experimental modelâs aerodynamics and scaling assumptions is insufficient.
Independent, dependent and control variables are not consolidated in a single clear table or paragraph.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands (topic clarity and research question focus) clearly meet the highest band descriptors, demonstrating sustained purpose and alignment. The methodology strand, however, remains only adequate due to unaddressed scaling and control-variable issues. Averaging the three strand performances yields a criterion mark just below the maximum, meriting a score of 5.

strengths: Solid application of circular motion theory, velocity and g-force derivations, and relevant secondary sources.
Consistent and precise use of disciplinary terminology (centripetal acceleration, RMSE, brake-by-wire, error propagation).
weaknesses: Conceptual gaps in the linear volume-to-g-force scaling and occasional unit slips undermine full conceptual accuracy.
Some key citations (e.g., physiological limits for g-force tolerance) are missing to support statements.
Minor typographical errors in variable names and equations detract from flawless presentation of knowledge.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Subject knowledge is good but not uniformly excellent (mid-band), while terminology use is nearly top-band. Weighting these two strands together yields a strong mid-band performance, justifying a score of 5 out of 6.

strengths: Purposeful data analysis linking model results to race telemetry with regression, RMSE and uncertainty propagation.
Well-structured argument that progresses logically from theory through experiment to conclusion.
Identification of practical limitations and realistic suggestions for methodological improvements.
weaknesses: Occasional over-reach in interpretation (e.g., extrapolating fatal outcomes without fully reconciling scaling flaws).
Reasoning gaps due to unexamined alternative explanations (aerodynamics, tyre grip) and late discussion of limitations.
Critical evaluation of the core scaling assumption remains limited and would benefit from deeper impact analysis on conclusions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis is generally focused and demonstrates good depth (7/12 band), argument development is coherent though occasionally inconsistent (5/12 band), and evaluation is solid but not exhaustive (5/12 band). The combined performance across these three strands averages to the lower half of the mid-to-upper bands, warranting a score of 6 out of 12.

strengths: Clear and appropriate overall structure with correct use of title page, contents, section numbering and references.
Effective layout with readable fonts, colour-coded graphs and cross-referenced figures that aid navigation.
Equations and tables are generally well-captioned and integrated into the narrative.
weaknesses: Some images are low-resolution and tables appear cluttered, detracting from polish.
Inconsistent spacing around equations and occasional misaligned figure/table borders.
Occasional orphaned captions and inconsistent figure sizing interrupt visual flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is competent and functional, meeting key structural and formatting conventions (upper band of 1-2), but minor lapses in layout and visual polish prevent it from reaching the top band. A score of 3 out of 4 reflects this level of performance.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Physics terminology is deployed accurately and fluently: centripetal acceleration, RMSE, brake-by-wire, error propagation and linearisation are all used in correct contexts.  Equations are formatted clearly and variables are defined.  

Apart from a rare typo (âq-forcesâ) and the previously noted scaling simplification, the language shows **consistent, precise command of disciplinary concepts**, justifying a high-band mark.

The argument follows a logical sequenceâbackground theory, experimental findings, race-data comparison, evaluationâand is usually coherent.  Claims are supported by data tables and graphs, and the conclusion addresses the RQ directly.  

However, some reasoning leaps (e.g., assuming direct proportionality between model and real-car g-loads) weaken the chain of logic, and the discussion of limitations appears late, reducing persuasive power.  Overall, the argument is **well-developed but exhibits moderate inconsistency**, appropriate for the upper half of the 4-6 band.

The procedure (string-whirl model, five radii, ten revolutions per trial, error propagation) is described in sufficient detail for replication and makes sensible use of primary and secondary data.  However, several methodological weaknesses remain:  
â¢ The 1â¶43 linear scaling of g-forces ignores aerodynamic and dimensional considerations, producing unphysical values (> 160 g).  
â¢ Control variables such as ambient conditions, string tension and initial angular speed are noted only briefly.  
â¢ Outlier handling (e.g., Trial 3 at 10 cm) is acknowledged but not systematically treated.  

These issues indicate an **adequate but not fully appropriate or complete** methodology, aligning with the 3-4 band.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of circular motion, scaling relations, and Formula 1 brake-by-wire technology.  Derivations from raw timing to velocity, acceleration and g-force are correct, and secondary sources are generally relevant.  

Nevertheless, the linear volume-to-g-force scaling and minor unit slips (misprinted circumferences) reveal conceptual gaps.  The application of subject knowledge is therefore **good but not consistently excellent**, meriting the mid-band score.

The student identifies methodological flaws (string catching wheels, timing accuracy, scaling assumptions) and proposes realistic improvements.  Error propagation is calculated and linked to data confidence.  

Still, evaluation of the core scaling assumption and its impact on the conclusions is limited, and external factors (e.g., driver technique, aerodynamic load) receive scant attention.  The work therefore shows a **good but not exhaustive critical evaluation**, meriting a solid mid-band score.

The studentâs topicâinvestigating how Formula 1 braking systems mitigate g-forcesââis introduced immediately, reiterated in the overview, and sign-posted in every major section (background, data analysis, conclusion).  Headings and transitional phrases keep the reader oriented and the 3 488-word limit is used efficiently.  

Key physics terms (centripetal acceleration, brake-by-wire) are woven into the narrative, and the purpose of each graph or table is stated directly beneath it.  Minor wording lapses (e.g., âerror propogationâ) do not prevent accurate communication.  Overall, the topic is conveyed with **clarity, precision and sustained focus**, fully matching the top-band descriptor.

The report employs a clean two-column contents page, readable fonts and colour-coded graphs that enhance comprehension.  Pagination and headings function correctly, and equations are typeset clearly.  

However, some images are low-resolution, a few tables are cluttered, and spacing around equations and figures is irregular.  While these issues do not obscure meaning, they prevent the layout from being exemplary.  The submission thus aligns with the **competent yet improvable upper-band score of 3**.

The research questionâââTo what extent do the design and implementation of braking systems contribute to reducing g-forces experienced by Formula 1 drivers during high-speed manoeuvres?âââis explicit, narrowly scoped and measurable.  It guides the entire investigation: each subsection (experimental model, scaling, race-data comparison) feeds directly into judging the braking systemâs impact.  

Throughout the discussion the student returns to the RQ, comparing scaled model values with on-track data to quantify the âextentâ.  Consequently, the essay displays **continuous alignment between research question, method, analysis and conclusion**, a hallmark of the highest band.

The essay chains raw data â processed data â comparative analysis with actual race telemetry, demonstrating purposeful analytical depth.  Regression, RMSE evaluation and propagated uncertainties are employed to interrogate relationships.  

Yet, interpretations occasionally over-reach (e.g., declaring fatal outcomes from model data without fully reconciling scaling flaws) and some alternative explanations (aerodynamic downforce, tyre grip) are not explored.  Thus the research analysis is **good and generally focused**, but not of consistently âexcellentâ calibre, fitting the lower end of the 7-9 band.

All formal EE elements (title page, abstract-style overview, contents list, section numbering, reference list) are present and largely consistent.  Tables and figures are clearly captioned and cross-referenced, aiding navigation.  

Nonetheless, small lapsesââmisaligned table borders, occasional orphaned captions, and inconsistent figure sizingââdetract from full polish.  These minor issues place the work in the **upper range of the appropriate-structure band (3-4)**.

annotations.comment: The contents page is clearly structured with hierarchical numbering and page references, aiding navigation.
Weaknesses section identifies practical issues; strengthen by quantifying their impact on final uncertainty or data quality.
The uncertainties discussion ends abruptly. Clarify how 0.005âm for radius was determined and its effect on g-force propagation.
The inclusion of a clear word count at the beginning demonstrates adherence to IB formatting requirements, ensuring the word limit is transparent.
The conclusion effectively compares experimental and race g-force values to answer the research question with clear quantitative contrasts.
The title is prominently stated, immediately orienting the reader to the investigationâs focus.
The opening paragraph provides general background on mechanics but could more directly align with the research question by focusing on g-force dynamics.
The description of scaling uses â<math>\frac{1}{43}</math>â notation. For clarity and consistency, enclose equations in $$â¦$$, e.g. $$\frac{1}{43}$$, and justify linear scaling assumptions.
Error propagation uses Îv/v without stating measurement uncertainties for string length and timing. Define these values explicitly.
Explain how the absence of brakes is implemented experimentally, ensuring the modelâs motion remains comparable to an F1 car without BBW.
When introducing $$a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}$$, explicitly define each variable with units and present as a block equation for clarity.
The centripetal acceleration values use mixed v values (2.95 vs 2.96âm/s). Ensure calculations use the same numbers reported in the velocity table.
Graph 1 lacks a trend line or equation of best fit; add it to visualise the relationship before linearization.
The model car is said to replicate aerodynamics; include a citation or data validating that its drag characteristics are comparable to F1 cars.
The anecdotal example of Alonsoâs crash is interesting but remains disconnected from the physics analysis. Link such cases explicitly to your g-force data.
The 40âcm g-force entry is missing a numeric result. Complete all table entries to avoid gaps in data.
The research question is explicit, narrowly focused and directly frames the investigationâs purpose.
Choice of turns one and thirteen is based on circular shape; strengthen this by quantifying turn radii and comparing to model radii explicitly.
Table 5 uses log values but doesnât specify log base. Indicate âlog$_{10}$â or âlnâ and include units or dimensionless label.
Terms like âBBWâ are well defined; ensure every acronym (e.g. BBW) is defined upon first use and consistently used thereafter.
The physiological limits of the human body are described. Cite primary literature for g-force tolerance (e.g. fighter pilot studies) to strengthen accuracy.
Independent, dependent and control variables should be explicitly listed in a single paragraph or table to improve methodological clarity.
Calculations for race g-forces assume a track radius of 15âm and 90âm without citation. State source or method for obtaining turn radii.
Graph 3 overlays turn 1 and 13 data but omits error bars. Include vertical uncertainties to compare variability.
Step 5 scales g-forces by a factor of 43 linearly. Re-examine scaling laws: g-force scales with linear dimension differently than volume.
Rounding inconsistency: circumference calculated as 0.63âm but used as 0.62âm in distance. Maintain consistent significant figures throughout.
Graph 2 presents three fits; clarify which line is used for quantitative analysis and discuss the rationale for selecting that slope.

Example equations are embedded in text blocks. Format them as standalone LaTeX displays (e.g.
$$v = \frac{6.3}{2.75}$$) for readability.
Inclusion of race screenshots with overlays effectively links experimental findings to real-world telemetry.
Discussion occasionally conflates statistical correlation with causation. Refine language to reflect that correlation suggests but does not prove causality.
The second paragraph of the conclusion ends mid-sentence. Complete thoughts to ensure clear, polished argumentation.
The evaluation strengths are descriptive; deepen critical insight by discussing how model limitations affect transferability of results.
Extensions propose new brake types; include preliminary methodology (e.g. model design, variables) to guide future work.
The reference list contains inconsistent formatting and broken links. Apply a uniform citation style (APA) with hanging indents and complete entries.
